name: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
subject: Chemistry

sections.heading: Classification and Nomenclature
sections.body:
Compounds with –OH on sp³ C are alcohols (R–OH); on sp² C of aromatic ring, phenols (Ar–OH). Ethers have R–O–R'.

Alcohols subdivided by the C bearing –OH:

  • Primary (1°): –OH on C with one C neighbour (CH3CH2OH).
  • Secondary (2°): –OH on C with two C neighbours.
  • Tertiary (3°): –OH on C with three C neighbours.

Also monohydric, dihydric (glycols), trihydric (glycerol) by number of –OH.

IUPAC: parent = longest chain containing –OH; suffix -ol; locant lowest for –OH. Phenols: hydroxy as prefix or use phenol/cresol etc. Ethers: the smaller R group + O is named alkoxy substituent (CH3OC2H5 = methoxyethane).

sections.heading: Preparation of Alcohols
sections.body:
From alkenes:

  • Acid-catalysed hydration (Markovnikov): CH2=CH2 + H2O / H2SO4 → CH3CH2OH.
  • Hydroboration–oxidation (anti-Markovnikov, syn): RCH=CH2 →(i) B2H6; (ii) H2O2/OH⁻ RCH2CH2OH.

From carbonyl compounds (reduction):

  • Aldehyde → 1° alcohol; Ketone → 2° alcohol. Reagents: H2/Ni, NaBH4, LiAlH4.
  • Carboxylic acid/ester → 1° alcohol with LiAlH4.

From Grignard reagents: HCHO + RMgX → 1° alcohol; RCHO + R'MgX → 2° alcohol; R2CO + R'MgX → 3° alcohol; CO2 + RMgX → carboxylic acid.

Phenol:

  • From cumene (Hock process): isopropylbenzene + O2 → cumene hydroperoxide → phenol + acetone (industrial).
  • From haloarenes (Dow process): C6H5Cl + NaOH (300°C, 200 atm) → C6H5ONa →H⁺ C6H5OH.
  • From benzene sulphonic acid: fuse with NaOH.
  • From diazonium salt: ArN2⁺ + H2O →warm ArOH + N2.

sections.heading: Physical Properties
sections.body:
Alcohols form intermolecular hydrogen bonds, giving anomalously high boiling points (compare CH3CH2OH 78 °C vs CH3OCH3 −24 °C with same molecular weight). B.p. of alcohols rises with molecular weight; for isomers, decreases with branching (1° > 2° > 3°).

Solubility in water: lower alcohols (C1–C3) are completely miscible due to H-bonding; solubility decreases with chain length (hydrocarbon part dominates). Phenols are slightly soluble in water.

Ethers have no –OH, so no inter-molecular H-bonding; their boiling points are similar to alkanes of comparable mass and far lower than alcohols. They can H-bond with water (slight solubility).

sections.heading: Acidic Character and Reactions Involving Cleavage of O–H
sections.body:
Order of acidity: carboxylic acid > phenol > water > alcohol. Phenols (pKa ≈ 10) are more acidic than alcohols (pKa ≈ 16) because the phenoxide ion is resonance-stabilised by the aromatic ring (negative charge delocalised onto ortho and para C atoms). Electron-withdrawing groups (–NO2, –Cl) increase acidity, especially at o/p positions; electron-donating groups (–CH3, –OCH3) decrease it.

Reactions of –OH:

  • With Na: 2 R–OH + 2 Na → 2 R–O⁻Na⁺ + H2 (alcohols and phenols).
  • With NaOH: only phenols react (alcohols too weak).
  • Esterification: R–OH + R'COOH ⇌ R'COOR + H2O (acid-catalysed; reversible). With acid chlorides or anhydrides, irreversible. Phenols give esters with acyl chlorides/anhydrides in presence of pyridine or NaOH.

sections.heading: Reactions Involving Cleavage of C–O of Alcohols
sections.body:
Reaction with HX: R–OH + HX → R–X + H2O. Lucas test (HCl/ZnCl2):

  • 3° alcohols give immediate cloudy solution.
  • 2° alcohols give cloudiness within 5 min.
  • 1° alcohols do not react at room T.

Reaction with PX3, PCl5, SOCl2: same as Ch 6 prep of haloalkanes. SOCl2 is preferred (gas byproducts).

Dehydration to alkenes: R–OH →conc. H2SO4, heat alkene + H2O. Reactivity: 3° > 2° > 1° (carbocation mechanism). Saytzeff product dominates. Also achievable with anhyd. Al2O3 at 350 °C (1°), with H3PO4 at 440 °C (2°). With ethanol + conc. H2SO4 at 140 °C, diethyl ether forms (intermolecular dehydration); at 170 °C ethene (intramolecular).

Oxidation:

  • 1° alcohol → aldehyde → carboxylic acid (with strong oxidant like KMnO4/K2Cr2O7). To stop at aldehyde, use PCC (pyridinium chlorochromate) in CH2Cl2, or Cu/573 K (gives aldehyde with dehydrogenation).
  • 2° alcohol → ketone.
  • 3° alcohol: no oxidation under normal conditions; with strong oxidant, ring/chain cleavage.

sections.heading: Phenol — Distinctive Reactions
sections.body:
Phenol's –OH is strongly activating and ortho/para directing in electrophilic aromatic substitution (lone pair donates into ring by resonance):

  • Halogenation: with Br2/H2O at room T → 2,4,6-tribromophenol (white precipitate). With Br2/CS2 at low T → mono-bromophenol (mostly p-).
  • Nitration: with dil. HNO3 → o- and p-nitrophenol (separated by steam distillation; o-isomer steam-volatile due to intramolecular H-bond). With conc. HNO3 → 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid).
  • Sulphonation: with conc. H2SO4; o-product favoured at 298 K, p-product at 373 K (kinetic vs thermodynamic).
  • Kolbe's reaction: phenoxide + CO2 (4–7 atm, 400 K) → salicylic acid (o-hydroxybenzoic acid). Acetylation gives aspirin.
  • Reimer–Tiemann reaction: phenoxide + CHCl3 + NaOH → salicylaldehyde (after acidic workup). Mechanism via dichlorocarbene :CCl2.
  • Reaction with zinc dust: C6H5OH + Zn → C6H6 + ZnO.
  • Oxidation with Na2Cr2O7/H2SO4 → benzoquinone.

sections.heading: Ethers — Preparation and Reactions
sections.body:
Williamson synthesis: best general method. R–O⁻Na⁺ + R'–X → R–O–R' + NaX. SN2 mechanism; works best with primary R'–X (3° gives elimination).

For unsymmetrical ethers, choose alkoxide of bulkier group + halide of smaller group: t-BuO⁻ + CH3I gives t-Bu–O–CH3; reverse (t-BuBr + CH3O⁻) gives mostly elimination → isobutylene.

From alcohols (intermolecular dehydration): 2 C2H5OH + H2SO4, 140 °C → C2H5OC2H5 + H2O. Only good for symmetrical 1° ethers.

Reactions of ethers:

  • Ethers are relatively inert: no reaction with bases, oxidants, or active metals at ordinary T.
  • Cleavage by HX (HI > HBr > HCl): R–O–R' + HX → R–X + R'–OH (or two halides if excess HX). For mixed alkyl–aryl ether (anisole + HI), Ar–O bond is stronger so ArOH + RI form; not RO + ArI. For dialkyl ethers, more substituted side gives R–I via SN1; less substituted via SN2.
  • Electrophilic substitution on aryl ethers: –OR is activator (o/p directing).

summary: Alcohols (R–OH) are 1°/2°/3° depending on the C bearing –OH; phenols have –OH on aromatic ring; ethers have R–O–R'. Alcohols are made from alkenes (acid hydration or hydroboration–oxidation), from carbonyls (NaBH_4, LiAlH_4), and via Grignard. Phenol is industrially made from cumene (Hock) and from chlorobenzene (Dow). H-bonding makes alcohols high-boiling and water-soluble; ethers behave like alkanes plus weak H-bond accept. Acidity order: carboxylic acid > phenol > water > alcohol; phenoxide is resonance-stabilised. Alcohols undergo Lucas test (3° > 2° > 1°), dehydration to alkenes (3° > 2° > 1°), oxidation (1° → ald → acid; 2° → ketone). Phenol is strongly activated for o/p EAS — Kolbe (→ salicylic acid) and Reimer–Tiemann (→ salicylaldehyde) are signature reactions. Ethers are made by Williamson synthesis (best with 1° halides) and cleaved by HX (HI > HBr).
pitfalls: Saying alcohols are stronger acids than phenols — phenols win by resonance stabilisation of phenoxide.
Predicting LiAlH_4 will reduce a C=C double bond — it doesn't (only polar C=O, C=N, etc.).
Trying Williamson synthesis with 3° halide — gives elimination, not ether.
Forgetting that with HI, anisole gives PhOH + CH_3I (Ar–O bond stronger), not C_6H_5I.
Not distinguishing intramolecular (170°C, ethene) vs intermolecular (140°C, diethyl ether) ethanol dehydration.
Using Br_2/H_2O on phenol expecting monobromination — get 2,4,6-tribromophenol; for monobromo, use Br_2/CS_2 at low T.
Confusing Reimer–Tiemann (gives ortho aldehyde) with Kolbe (gives ortho carboxylic acid).
